reviews.review_text: Great sandbox game. Much improved over original Mount & Blade, and with a simplicity Bannerlord lacks.

Enter the game.
Complete missions.
Participate in the tournament. 
Get captured by bandits.
Escape. 
Gather soldiers. 
Get captured by bandits. 
Escape. 
Enter your first battle. 
Lose. 
Great game!
one of the best games of all times, it was groundbreaking at the time, and is still amazing, you can endlessly sink your teeth into this gem.

Greatest medieval sandbox ever made. Keep coming back to it after all this time. As it stands, I still prefer it to Bannerlord. Genuinely feels like one of those quintessential PC games everyone has to try at least once

I forget how good this game is. Used to play it a lot on a mediocre laptop, traveling with no internet.

I treat this game as a Arena Brawlers with some management mechanics. X-Com with swords.

I've been returning to Warband for years, accumulating 500+ hours on Steam alone. I suppose it's finally time to write a review.

What Warband does best is deliver the feeling of an 80s medieval action movie. Fellow Turkish players will understand when I say I feel like Tarkan or Cüneyt Arkın while playing.

The core gameplay loop is simple but addictive. You recruit troops, travel between towns and castles, fight battles, and gradually build your power. The strategic layer isn't particularly deep, and both politics and fief management are fairly shallow -arguably by design- but that's also why the game stays focused on what matters most: fighting. If you know what you're doing, you can pull off ridiculous hero moments and win battles that feel like 1 vs. 30.

One weakness is that the game's freedom is somewhat limited in the long run. You can spend dozens of hours as a mercenary, trader, raider, or wandering adventurer, but eventually the game pushes you toward becoming a vassal, founding a kingdom, or simply ending your campaign. Alternative playstyles exist, but they don't receive the same level of support.

The soundtrack is excellent, and the artwork has aged remarkably well. The graphics were good for their time and, while obviously dated, they still don't make your eyes bleed today.

The modding scene is another huge strength. With the right mods, Warband can feel like a completely different game and offer hundreds of additional hours of content.

If you're looking for deep kingdom management or grand strategy, this isn't it. But if you want to live out a medieval action movie fantasy where you start with nothing and rise to power while carving through armies as a legendary warrior, Warband still delivers.
Love this game!!!  Real fighting, managing toops, upgrading, managing towns, customization, weapon/armour upgrade, completing tasks.   It's got everything!

amazing. the gameplay feels so clean, so coherent. 

playing this I feel like I am reading the golden age arc from Berserk

Hmm. Hard to review this because it's so old and obviously some things have aged better than others. I had a good bit of fun, but the novelty has worn off, and now its just a slog. The combat can be fun, and the troop training/progression system is cool. The early game grind is challenging but feasible and rewarding. Mods like PoP are outstanding, leaves almost no reason to play vanilla. However, many things are extremely grind heavy, and the AI is outrageously bad (both combat and campaign). When campaigning there's a good chance the vassals will abandon the Marshall halfway through a siege, and even if the siege is successful, they all immediately depart for a feast halfway across the map leaving the newly captured town completely undefended. For how pointless feasts are in the mid game, this is a pretty glaring oversight in the lord AI that makes conquering feel like the worst group assignment from your college nightmares. If I could have a big enough army to conquer on my own I absolutely would, but your party size is capped significantly lower than the NPC lords which doesn't really make sense to me - recruiting a big enough army to siege is tough enough, but forcing you to conquer with other parties makes it a truly infuriating experience. Especially without the script extender, large battles do not play out fairly even with battle advantage. Reinforcement waves are simply too far away on the gigantic maps, so the fight oscillates back and forth, with one side sweeping the other for a wave or two before getting pushed back to their spawn, where the same thing happens for upwards of 15 minutes. Auto-resolve is far less painful but defeats the central appeal of the game. Good for small army fights, terrible for 90% of the fights you'll get into in late game, siege fights being perhaps the only exception.

I played a good bit of Viking Conquest which, for the time, was still an outstanding accomplishment in terms of its breadth, but a lot of the content is fairly shallow, and some kingdoms are just unfinished. At the end of the day you're still gonna spend a bunch of time doing boring ♥♥♥♥ for leaders to progress with your faction, like hunting down bandit dens (which can be as far as 100 miles away, unmarked on the map) instead of doing the fun stuff (siege fights, hunting vikings on the open sea, making scandalous amounts of money selling wool in Dorestad). Since I got hard stuck on the "Conqueror" quest on multiple playthroughs of VC, I tried out the Ireland quest. This was fine until I had to increase the relationship of the town in Gwynedd even after giving the mayor 5000 smackeroons, seemingly for nothing. He wanted me to search through 9 villages to find a stupid lute. Even at my wits end, playing with cheat menu enabled to teleport around the map and reveal bandit camps, this final quest really sent me over the edge. Without cheats, that would have probably taken upwards of half an hour of the most boring useless questing, just to get a tip on where to go for the next step of the quest. No. Thanks. I had my fun, but I'm done. 

It's a shame it doesn't seem like Bannerlord is much better - combat system has improved but the lack of modding support and their use of ♥♥♥♥♥♥ AI in the new DLC more than offsets that. Oh well. Guess I should try KCD or something.

Yes, it's old and the rather good sequel is out, but for me it's still worth it for the mods.

The game still boasts:
A unique gameplay loop that blends strategy, management and action.
The graphics are old, not bad IMO. They have a style that can be further customized by the mods I mentioned.
Since you can play it in multiple ways, this game can satisfy different gaming cravings. You can focus on diplomacy and management and get a completely different experience too where you are focused on fighting.
Amazing mods and great time spender! I do wish they had a carthage vs rome mod as its a big game.

I can wholeheartedly say that Mount & and Blade: Warband is one of the greatest Medieval games. 

I am upset that I can't easily acquire all the achievements for it due to some being locked behind Multiplayer which is no existent aside from a few Mods like Full Invasion 2: Orisis have 1-2 active dedicated servers.

Regardless, one of my favorite and most played games in my library.
The base game is good, but the mods make it shine. A wonderful game for the ages.

Few games have managed to achieve what Mount & Blade: Warband has. More than a decade after its release, it remains a beloved classic, not merely because of what the developers created, but because it was blessed with one of the most talented, passionate, and creative modding communities in gaming. Together, the base game and its countless community-made expansions have kept Warband alive for years, transforming it from an already unique medieval sandbox into a near-limitless platform for adventure.

At its core, Warband offers something few games can replicate: the feeling of truly living in a war-torn medieval world. The land of Calradia is divided between rival kingdoms locked in constant conflict, and your character is thrown into this turbulent landscape with little more than ambition and opportunity. What follows is a journey that is entirely your own and I think this proved to be a pure gold approach.

You can become a ruthless bandit, preying upon vulnerable villages and terrorizing merchants traveling the roads. You can ambush caravans, disrupting trade routes and damaging the flow of goods between cities. If glory is what you seek, you can dedicate yourself to tournaments, earning fame and fortune as a renowned knight celebrated for your martial prowess. You can choose a more honorable path as well, helping villagers with their troubles, protecting travelers, and building a reputation as a trustworthy and noble individual. Or perhaps your ambitions reach even higher: swear fealty to a king, prove your loyalty on the battlefield, rise through the ranks of the nobility, and eventually lead armies in a campaign to conquer all of Calradia.

The beauty of Warband lies in the fact that all of these paths, and many more, are possible. The game rarely dictates who you should be, instead allowing your actions and decisions to shape your story. Massive cavalry charges, desperate castle sieges, political maneuvering, economic ventures, and personal rivalries all combine to create countless memorable moments that emerge naturally through gameplay.

That said, Warband is not a flawless game. The vanilla experience can feel somewhat limited by modern standards. While the various lifestyles and career paths are present, they are not always explored with the depth or complexity that contemporary RPG players might expect. Many systems remain relatively simple, and some activities can become repetitive over time. Visually, the game also shows its age. The graphics were modest even at release and are undeniably dated today.

Yet these shortcomings rarely diminish what makes Warband special. The strength of its sandbox design, the freedom it grants the player, and the emergent stories it creates continue to outweigh its technical limitations. More importantly, the modding community has spent years expanding virtually every aspect of the game. From historical overhauls and fantasy total conversions to deep gameplay reworks that add entirely new mechanics, Warband's mods elevate an already excellent game into something truly extraordinary.

This game is awesome. I bought it a long time ago but didn't get into it because I played the tutorial and was confused on how to play the game. My recommendation is to not play the tutorial. It is more of a gun range than a tutorial which shows all the weapons but does not explain how to play. You want to first get a good group of soldiers going and then align with a kingdom. sieging castles and towns is one of the most enjoyable experiences I have had while playing a game.

Over 16 years after its release and Warband is still kicking, in large part due to the amazing modding community. Baller game, definitely worth the purchase, would highly recommend using WSE2 to run it though. 
10/10 game

Now I didn't know about this game when it came out so this is said with something of the freshness of the the experience to it: With all the mods for this game I have lived a hundred different stories in different settings. I got the full experience of fighting in a shield wall and firing in square with riflemen. I've dueled Khalid bin Walid and I've partied with Moff Tarkin. As a history enthusiast it helps with visualization of obscure periods. There are a lot of things that are a little 'dated' about this game, but to me it's just right in terms of features vs clunkiness, performance, etc. I wish stuff like issuing orders was a lot easier, but for now it is nowhere near ruining the fun I'm having. Thanks to the incredible community!
